exercises.title: The three files of the minimum viable workbench
exercises.prompt: The smallest useful workbench is three files: a root instructions router, a state file, and a task board. Define each and explain why a short root router beats a long monolithic AGENTS.md.
exercises.hint: Root router: a short index that points to where details live (don't dump everything). State file: current state the agent reads every turn. Task board: tasks to do/in-progress/done. Short router beats long AGENTS.md because the router is always-loaded (context budget) and routes to details loaded on demand.
exercises.solution:
**The three files of the minimum viable workbench:**
1. **Root instructions router:** a **short index/router** at the repo root that **points to where details live** — 'for scope rules see X, for the state file see Y, for how to run tests see Z'. It's a **lightweight router**, not a dump of all instructions. The agent reads it first and follows the pointers to load only what it needs.
2. **State file:** the **current durable state** the agent **reads at every turn and writes at the end** — what's been done, key decisions, the current situation (the durable repo memory, Lesson 34). It's the agent's persistent memory of the work, in a versioned file.
3. **Task board:** the **tasks** — what's **to do, in progress, and done**. It tracks the work items and their status, so the agent (and the next session) knows what's outstanding and what's complete.
These three — **router (where to find things), state (what's happened), task board (what to do)** — are the **minimum viable workbench.** 'If a repo cannot carry these three, no model will save it' — they're the irreducible foundation; everything else (scope contracts, verification, etc.) layers on top.

**Why a short root router beats a long monolithic AGENTS.md:** 
- **Context budget (the key reason):** the root instructions are **loaded into the agent's context on every turn/session** — so a **long monolithic AGENTS.md** (everything dumped in one big file) **consumes huge context every time** (Phase 11.5, Lesson 1's token limits), crowding out the actual task and degrading the model (context rot, Phase 11.5). A **short router** is **cheap to always-load** — it's just an index — and it **points to details that are loaded *on demand*** (only when relevant). This is **progressive disclosure** (Phase 13.22's SKILL.md pattern, Lesson 10's skill retrieval) — load the cheap index always, load the expensive details only when needed. The router keeps the always-loaded context lean.
- **Maintainability:** a long monolithic file becomes **unmaintainable** (everything tangled together); a **router + separate detail files** is **modular** (each concern in its own file, the router indexes them) — easier to update, version, and reason about.
- **Relevance:** the router lets the agent **load only the relevant details for the current task** (follow the right pointer), rather than wading through everything irrelevant in a monolith.
- **Routing > dumping:** a router **organizes and directs** (here's where each thing is); a monolith **dumps** (here's everything, find what you need). Routing is the better information architecture — it respects the context budget and improves relevance.
The principle (Phase 11.5, 13.22, Lesson 10): **keep the always-loaded context minimal (a short router) and load details on demand (progressive disclosure)** — a long monolithic AGENTS.md violates this by forcing all instructions into context every turn. The short root router is the **context-efficient** way to give the agent its instructions — point to details, don't dump them.

exercises.title: Building a state file the agent reads and writes
exercises.prompt: Build a state file the agent reads at every turn and writes at the end. Define what belongs in it, sketch its structure, and explain the read-at-start/write-at-end discipline. Why does the state file go in the repo (versioned), not just chat history?
exercises.hint: State file: current task, what's done, key decisions, blockers, next steps — structured (JSON/markdown). Read at every turn start (orient), write at end (persist progress). In the repo because chat history is volatile (lost when session ends); the repo is durable, versioned, shared across sessions/agents/reviewers.
exercises.solution:
**What belongs in the state file:** the **durable current state of the work** — enough for any session to orient and continue:
- **Current task / goal:** what's being worked on.
- **What's been done:** progress so far (completed steps, what's working).
- **Key decisions:** important choices made and *why* (so they're not relitigated/forgotten).
- **Blockers / open questions:** what's stuck or uncertain.
- **Next steps:** what to do next.
- **Relevant context:** key facts/paths discovered (so they're not re-derived).
```json
{
  "current_task": "add input validation to the API",
  "done": ["added schema", "wired the validator"],
  "decisions": ["used Pydantic for validation (matches existing code)"],
  "blockers": ["the auth test is flaky — needs investigation"],
  "next_steps": ["add tests for the empty-input case", "run the full suite"],
  "context": {"test_command": "pytest tests/", "entry_point": "src/api.py"}
}
```
Structured (JSON or markdown) so it's **parseable and consistent** — the agent reads and updates it reliably (and a schema can validate it, Lesson 34).

**The read-at-start/write-at-end discipline:**
- **Read at every turn/session start:** the agent **reads the state file first** to **orient** — 'what's the task, what's done, what's next, what did I decide?' This grounds the agent in the durable state **before acting**, so it continues coherently (not starting blind or re-deriving context). Critical for **continuity across turns and sessions** (and after context loss, Lesson 26).
- **Write at the end (and at key points):** the agent **updates the state file** with its progress — what it did, new decisions, updated next-steps — so the **state reflects reality** and the **next turn/session reads accurate state.** Persisting progress is what makes the state *durable* (survives the session ending).
The discipline — **read to orient, act, write to persist** — turns the volatile in-context work into **durable, continuable state.** Every turn: load state → do work → save state. This is the agent's memory loop (the MemGPT page-in/page-out pattern, Lesson 7, simplified to a repo file).

**Why the state file goes in the repo (versioned), not just chat history:** 
- **Chat history is volatile; the repo is durable:** **chat history is lost when the session ends** (or gets truncated as context fills, Phase 11.5, Lesson 7's finite-context wall) — it's **ephemeral**. The **repo is durable** — files persist across sessions, survive crashes, and are always available. So state in the repo **survives the session** (Lesson 34's 'chat history is volatile, the repo is durable').
- **Versioned (git):** in the repo, the state file is **version-controlled** — you can **see how state evolved, diff it, and roll back** (an audit trail of the agent's progress). Chat history has no versioning.
- **Shared across sessions, agents, and reviewers (one source of truth):** because it's in the repo, the **next session, a different agent, AND the human/reviewer all read the same state** (Lesson 34's 'same source of truth'). State in chat history is **trapped in one session** — invisible to the next session, other agents, or reviewers. Repo state is **the shared, durable source of truth** that enables handoff (Lesson 40), review (Lesson 39), and multi-agent collaboration.
- **Inspectable:** a human can open the state file and see exactly what the agent knows/did (transparency).
The principle (Lesson 34): **durable agent state belongs in the repo (versioned files), not in volatile chat history** — because the repo persists, versions, and is shared, while chat history is ephemeral, session-trapped, and lost. Putting state in the repo is what makes the workbench's memory **durable, continuable, and shared** — the foundation for handoff, review, and reliable multi-session work. (The next-level version: JSON schemas + a state manager with atomic persistence, Lesson 34.)

exercises.title: Why three files, the task board's role, and layering the full workbench
exercises.prompt: Explain why these three files are the minimum (what each provides that the others don't), the task board's role in scoping and continuity, why 'if a repo cannot carry these three, no model will save it', and how the full workbench (Lessons 33-40) layers on this minimal foundation.
exercises.hint: Router = where to find things (orientation/instructions). State = what's happened (memory). Task board = what to do (work tracking). Each is irreducible — remove one and the agent loses orientation, memory, or direction. 'Can't carry these three' = the repo lacks the basic scaffolding for ANY reliable agent work. Full workbench adds scope, feedback, verification, review, handoff on top.
exercises.solution:
**Why these three files are the minimum (what each uniquely provides):** the three files cover the **three irreducible needs** of any agent doing repo work — and **removing any one breaks the agent:**
- **Root router → orientation / instructions ('where things are, what the rules are'):** without it, the agent **doesn't know where to find anything or what the rules are** — it can't orient. It provides the *map* and the *rules*.
- **State file → memory ('what's happened'):** without it, the agent has **no durable memory** — it loses context across turns/sessions (context loss, Lesson 26), re-derives everything, contradicts past decisions. It provides *continuity*.
- **Task board → direction ('what to do'):** without it, the agent **doesn't know what work is outstanding or done** — no sense of progress or what's next. It provides *work tracking*.
Each provides something the others don't: **the router orients (where/rules), the state remembers (what happened), the task board directs (what to do).** Together they give the agent **orientation + memory + direction** — the minimum to work coherently over time. Remove the router → can't orient; remove state → no memory (context loss); remove the task board → no direction. They're **irreducible** — the smallest set that supports reliable agent work.

**The task board's role in scoping and continuity:**
- **Scoping:** the task board **defines the work items** — breaking the work into **discrete, trackable tasks** with status (to do / in progress / done). This **bounds and structures** the work (a precursor to scope contracts, Lesson 36) — the agent works on **defined tasks**, not an unbounded 'do stuff', reducing scope creep (Lesson 26). It makes the work **explicit and finite.**
- **Continuity:** the task board **carries the work forward across turns and sessions** — the next session reads the board and knows **exactly what's outstanding and what's done** (no re-figuring-out what to work on). It's the **work-tracking half of continuity** (the state file is the context half) — together they let the next session **pick up exactly where the last left off** (the foundation for handoff, Lesson 40). The board turns 'a pile of work' into 'a tracked list of tasks with status' — essential for multi-session continuity and not losing/duplicating work.

**Why 'if a repo cannot carry these three, no model will save it':** these three files are the **basic scaffolding for ANY reliable agent work** — they're the **floor**, not a nice-to-have:
- If the repo can't carry a **router**, the agent **can't orient** (doesn't know the rules/structure) — it works blind.
- If it can't carry **state**, the agent **has no durable memory** — it loses context and continuity every session (the structural context-loss failure, Lesson 26, unmitigated).
- If it can't carry a **task board**, the agent **has no direction or continuity** — it doesn't know what to do or what's done.
Without these three, the agent is **orientationless, memoryless, and directionless** — and **no model's capability fixes that** (Lesson 31: capability ≠ reliability; the structural failures, Lesson 26, go unmitigated). A frontier model **without orientation, memory, or direction** still **drifts, forgets, and loses the thread** — capability can't substitute for the missing scaffolding. So 'no model will save it' — if the repo lacks the **minimum workbench**, even the best model produces unreliable work (Lesson 31's thesis at the floor). The three files are the **non-negotiable foundation** — the minimum below which no model succeeds reliably.

**How the full workbench (Lessons 33-40) layers on this minimal foundation:** the three files are the **base**; the full workbench **layers the remaining surfaces on top:**
- **Instructions as executable constraints (Lesson 33):** the router's rules become **machine-checkable constraints** (not just prose) — a rule checker scores runs.
- **Repo memory and durable state (Lesson 34):** the state file gets **schemas + a state manager** (atomic, validated persistence) — the production version of the minimal state file.
- **Initialization scripts (Lesson 35):** automate the per-session orientation work (probe the repo, write findings to state) — so the agent doesn't redo it.
- **Scope contracts (Lesson 36):** per-task boundaries (allowed/forbidden files, acceptance criteria) — the formalized, verifiable version of the task board's scoping.
- **Runtime feedback loops (Lesson 37):** capture real command output into state — ground the agent in facts.
- **Verification gates (Lesson 38):** check the work against scope/rules/feedback before accepting — the agent doesn't self-certify.
- **Reviewer agent (Lesson 39):** a separate loop grades the work.
- **Multi-session handoff (Lesson 40):** package state for the next session.
Each surface **builds on the three files** — scope contracts extend the task board's scoping, the state manager extends the state file, verification reads the state/feedback, handoff packages the state. The full workbench is **the three-file foundation + the layered surfaces** (scope, feedback, verification, review, handoff). The synthesis: the **minimum viable workbench is three files (router, state, task board)** providing the irreducible **orientation + memory + direction** that any reliable agent work requires — 'if a repo can't carry these three, no model will save it' (capability can't substitute for the missing scaffolding, Lesson 31). The **full workbench (Lessons 33-40) layers the remaining surfaces (executable constraints, durable state managers, init scripts, scope contracts, feedback loops, verification gates, reviewer, handoff) on this foundation** — and the whole thing composes into the **reusable pack** (Lessons 41-42). Start with the three files (the floor); layer the surfaces (the reliability engineering); ship the pack. The minimal workbench is where reliable agent engineering begins.
